fast.summary: This systematic review evaluates real-world adherence rates to recommended blood-based screening tests (diabetes, dyslipidemia, HCV, HIV, PSA) to set expectations for blood-based colorectal cancer screening adoption. Adherence was highly variable and generally suboptimal, ranging from ~34% for HCV to ~68% for dyslipidemia screening.
fast.key_idea: Real-world adherence to blood-based screening tests is substantially lower than assumed, undermining the premise that blood tests inherently drive higher patient compliance compared to other screening modalities.
fast.why_it_matters: As blood-based multi-cancer early detection tests enter clinical practice, practitioners and health systems must not assume high uptake by default—targeted interventions to improve adherence will be critical for these tests to deliver population-level health benefits.
possible_use_cases: Benchmarking expected adherence rates when designing clinical trials or health economic models for new blood-based CRC screening tests
Identifying patient subgroups with historically low screening adherence to prioritize outreach and equity-focused interventions
Informing payer and policy decisions about coverage requirements and adherence support programs for blood-based cancer screening

deep.method_breakdown: This systematic review searched PubMed, Embase, and citation lists for studies published 2010–2023 examining real-world adherence to USPSTF grade A/B blood-based screening tests across four conditions (type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, HCV, HIV) plus PSA testing. Inclusion required venipuncture-derived blood tests and US-relevant USPSTF-recommended indications. Adherence rates were aggregated and summarized using medians and interquartile ranges across 69 included studies drawn from an initial pool of 53,067 articles.
deep.key_innovation: The novel contribution is using established blood-based screening adherence benchmarks as a proxy baseline to calibrate expectations for emerging blood-based colorectal cancer (CRC) screening tests. Prior work evaluated adherence for individual conditions in isolation; this review is the first to systematically aggregate cross-condition blood-test adherence to generate a generalizable real-world expectation for any new blood-based screening modality.
deep.technical_insight: The most important design choice is restricting inclusion to venipuncture-derived tests only, which eliminates stool-based and imaging-based CRC screening comparators and isolates the blood-draw-specific adherence signal. This matters because blood draw involves a distinct patient experience (clinical visit, phlebotomy) that may affect adherence differently than home-based or radiologic tests, making the benchmark more directly applicable to novel liquid biopsy CRC tests.
deep.limitations: Adherence definitions were heterogeneous across studies, making direct comparison difficult. Information and selection bias were identified as common risks, likely inflating adherence estimates. All data are US-centric, limiting global generalizability. The review cannot establish whether any observed adherence patterns would transfer to CRC blood-based screening given differences in disease salience, screening context, and patient motivation. PSA inclusion is complicated by its grade C recommendation and known controversy, which may introduce noise into the benchmark.
interview_questions: Why did the authors choose to include PSA testing despite its grade C USPSTF recommendation, and what does this methodological choice imply?
The median adherence for HCV and HIV screening (~34–37%) is much lower than for diabetes and dyslipidemia (~66–68%). What structural factors could explain this gap?
How should developers of blood-based CRC screening tests use these adherence benchmarks when modeling clinical utility or cost-effectiveness?
What are the key methodological pitfalls in measuring 'adherence' to screening tests, and how do they affect interpretation of these results?

If real-world adherence to blood-based CRC screening mirrors the HCV/HIV range (~34–37%) rather than the diabetes/dyslipidemia range (~66–68%), what are the downstream implications for population-level cancer mortality reduction?
interview_answers: PSA was included because it is the only widely available blood-based single-cancer screening test for average-risk adults, making it the most direct analog to a future blood-based CRC test. Despite the grade C recommendation (reflecting net benefit uncertainty rather than harm), PSA represents real-world clinical behavior around cancer-specific blood screening. The implication is that the benchmark may be optimistic if CRC blood tests receive similarly mixed guideline endorsement.

HCV and HIV screening targets populations defined by risk factors or birth cohorts rather than universal age-based criteria, which may reduce physician prompting and patient awareness. Stigma associated with infectious disease testing, particularly HIV, likely suppresses uptake. Diabetes and dyslipidemia screening is more deeply embedded in routine annual wellness visits and metabolic panels, making it nearly automatic in clinical workflow, whereas HCV/HIV requires a deliberate, separate order.

Developers should use the median adherence range (34–68%) as a scenario range in cost-effectiveness models rather than assuming high adherence. Given that CRC screening already has established non-blood alternatives (colonoscopy, FIT), adherence to a blood-based option will depend heavily on how it is positioned relative to existing options and whether guidelines unambiguously recommend it. Models assuming >70% adherence should be treated with skepticism absent strong real-world evidence.

Key pitfalls include heterogeneous definitions of adherence (ordered vs. completed vs. follow-up), variable observation windows, reliance on administrative claims data which may undercount uninsured populations, and selection bias from studying insured or high-engagement patient cohorts. These issues likely overestimate true population-level adherence, meaning published medians should be treated as upper-bound estimates for average-risk community populations.

If CRC blood-based screening adherence settles in the 34–37% range, the absolute reduction in CRC mortality would be substantially lower than modeled under optimistic adherence assumptions, potentially undermining cost-effectiveness thresholds. It would also raise health equity concerns if low-adherence groups overlap with already-underserved populations. This could shift the policy calculus toward investing in adherence infrastructure rather than test development alone.
